does: This paper systematically characterizes the relationship between sparse autoencoder width and causal influence on model outputs by introducing causal dimensionality kappa(L, M, T), defined as the effective rank of the expected Jacobian outer product at a given layer. The authors measure this quantity across seven SAE widths (16,384 to 1,048,576 features) on Gemma-2-2B layer 12 using SAE width sweeps paired with attribution patching. They find that representational capacity grows 15.6x while causal capacity grows only 4.35x, revealing a robust separation called the representational-causal wedge. Fitted estimates yield kappa approximately 1,990 (0.86 relative to d_model) with a participation-ratio lower bound of 280. Crucially, they demonstrate that causal dimensionality is invariant to model scaling: Gemma-2-9B and Gemma-2-2B produce identical causal structure despite a 3.46x parameter difference, establishing that the essential causal geometry is preserved across model sizes.
connects: This work builds on the sparse autoencoder literature that decomposes transformer activations into interpretable features, extending prior research that established SAEs as tools for understanding neural network representations. It connects to the mechanistic interpretability tradition by introducing quantitative measures of feature importance beyond simple reconstruction accuracy. The use of attribution patching links to prior causal analysis methods in deep learning that identify functionally critical components. The paper also engages with literature on effective dimensionality and intrinsic rank in neural networks, providing a principled framework for understanding the gap between representation space dimensionality and actual causal influence. By measuring how representational richness (captured by SAE width) diverges from causal impact, it addresses a fundamental question implicit in prior work on feature importance and saliency.
enables: This framework enables more precise characterization of which dimensions of neural network representations actually matter for model behavior, potentially guiding future interpretability efforts toward genuinely causal features. The invariance of causal dimensionality to model scaling suggests that fundamental structure in how transformers process information is stable across capacity—a finding that could inform scaling laws and architectural design. Future work can use these measurements to understand which features are redundant versus critical, improve interventional experiments on transformers, and develop more efficient models by identifying unnecessary representational capacity. The methodology also opens investigation into how causal dimensionality changes across layers, datasets, and tasks, potentially revealing universal principles of neural computation. Understanding the representational-causal wedge may guide compression techniques that reduce model size while preserving causal structure essential for performance.
